summary: Big moves in Reynoldstown as that long-stalled development near the BeltLine finally breaks ground, while Decatur gets a fresh take on affordable housing. Meanwhile, the airport chaos continues with ICE agents deployed to help with those brutal TSA lines.

topStory.headline: That Stalled Reynoldstown Corner Near BeltLine Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After five years of sitting empty, construction has finally started on 85 residential units at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace. The site was cleared back in 2021 but languished until Atlanta-based Embry Development took it over from Toll Brothers. It's about a block from Memorial Drive and just west of the ever-popular Eastside Trail, so expect this to move fast once momentum builds.
topStory.body:
You know that corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace that's been an empty lot forever? Well, construction equipment finally showed up. After nearly five years of sitting cleared and lonely, the 85-unit development is actually happening.

This whole saga started when the previous buildings got demolished back in 2021. Toll Brothers had big plans but ended up selling to Atlanta-based Embry Development two years ago. The new owners are moving forward with a mix of townhomes and condos, keeping the original permits but adding their own vision to the project.

What makes this spot interesting is the location. You're about a block north of Memorial Drive and just west of the Eastside Trail, where foot traffic never stops. With all the energy flowing through that corridor these days, this development should find its audience pretty quickly once units hit the market.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site with equipment at the corner of Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown

The site was cleared back in 2021 but languished until Atlanta-based Embry Development took it over from Toll Brothers. It's about a block from Memorial Drive and just west of the ever-popular Eastside Trail, so expect this to move fast once momentum builds.
stories.body:

This whole saga started when the previous buildings got demolished back in 2021. Toll Brothers had big plans but ended up selling to Atlanta-based Embry Development two years ago. stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Housing Village Opens Next Week
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is launching its soft opening with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards on Midway Road. With Decatur home values hitting $700,000, this mixed-income community aims to offer more accessible single-family options near downtown. The pocket neighborhood sits on 2.1 acres that were previously underused lots.
stories.body:
With home prices in Decatur pushing past $700k, finding anything affordable and family-friendly has become nearly impossible. That's what makes Cottages at Midway such a big deal. This 18-house village is designed around the 'missing middle' concept, creating more accessible homeownership options in a market that's priced out most working families.

The development sits on Midway Road where two underused lots totaled 2.1 acres. Instead of cramming in maximum density, the developers went for a village feel with shared courtyards and greenspace. It's the kind of setup that feels more like a neighborhood than a typical subdivision.

What's smart about this approach is the location. You're close to major commercial corridors and downtown Decatur, but the shared spaces and thoughtful design create a buffer from all that activity. If this model works, expect to see more of these pocket neighborhoods popping up in other ITP communities where land costs make traditional development challenging.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing layout of Cottages at Midway village development

stories.headline: West End's BeltLine Townhomes Finally Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is bringing 23 for-sale units to the West End near the BeltLine. Developer Ackerman & Co., which also owns Lee + White, started this project a year ago and expects the first seven properties to hit the market this spring. These are some of the rare for-sale townhomes along the BeltLine's southwest stretch.
stories.body:
The West End stretch of the BeltLine hasn't seen nearly as much residential development as the eastside, which makes GW Parkside kind of a big deal. Ackerman & Co. (the same folks behind Lee + White) are bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to Hopkins Street, and the first phase is finally looking like actual houses.

Demolition started a year ago on the 600 Hopkins Street site, but construction has been moving steadily since then. The first seven units should hit the market this spring, with the remaining 16 to follow. What's notable is that these are for-sale townhomes, not rentals, which gives people an ownership option in a neighborhood that's been mostly overlooked by developers.

The location puts you right in the mix of everything Lee + White has going on, plus easy BeltLine access. As the West End continues to evolve, having quality ownership housing options like this could help stabilize the neighborhood and give longtime residents a path to benefit from all the investment flowing into the area.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes development in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The menu features soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa, umami fries, and Peking chicken platters alongside cocktails like Thai tea martinis. Both chefs bring serious restaurant experience, with Jimenez having worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York.
stories.body:
After years of working in other people's kitchens, chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez finally have their own spot. Mushi Ni opened yesterday on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail, and it's exactly the kind of Asian-American comfort food this neighborhood needed.

The menu hits all the right notes. Think soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa, and those crab Rangoon puffs that disappear the moment they hit the table. The Peking chicken platter feeds two for just $25, which is a steal in today's market. And the cocktail program is serious too, with drinks like that Thai tea martini that somehow makes perfect sense.

What's impressive is the pedigree behind this place. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, so the execution is dialed in from day one. The location is smart too, catching foot traffic from the trail while serving a neighborhood that's always hungry for something new and well-done.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue's Texas-Style Smokehouse Opens at Ansley Mall
stories.summary: Pitmaster John Lewis brought his acclaimed Austin barbecue to the new Ansley Mall development behind The Cook's Warehouse. The upstairs smokehouse features six 22-foot smokers running 24/7, producing some of the best brisket in Atlanta. The location includes a pedestrian bridge connection to the BeltLine, making it easily accessible for trail users.
stories.body:
The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue is unlike anything you'll see in Atlanta. Most places hide their cookers in back rooms, but John Lewis put his six massive smokers upstairs in a glass-walled loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new BeltLine bridge. It's part factory, part theater, and the brisket coming out of those custom-welded smokers is absolutely worth the show.

Lewis helped build these 22-foot smokers himself out of old propane tanks, and they run in staggered eight-hour shifts around the clock. The result is that perfect bark and smoke ring that made his Austin location famous. This isn't Atlanta barbecue trying to be Texas barbecue, this is the real thing transported here by someone who knows exactly what they're doing.

The Ansley Mall location is brilliant too. You can walk over from the BeltLine via that new pedestrian bridge, grab some of the best barbecue in the city, and head back to the trail. It's the kind of destination that makes the BeltLine feel more complete as an actual transportation network rather than just a recreational path.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls While Firefighter Contract Festers
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions barring ICE detention facilities in city limits and requiring documentation when ICE agents are present during police operations. Meanwhile, dozens of firefighters and supporters criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the first-ever contract with the Atlanta firefighters union, which council ratified nearly a year ago.
stories.body:
Atlanta's City Council moved forward on two fronts this week, advancing measures to limit ICE operations while firefighters continue pushing Mayor Dickens on their stalled contract. The Public Safety Committee unanimously approved resolutions that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and require Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations.

But the bigger drama came from the packed gallery, where dozens of firefighters and supporters showed up to blast the mayor for refusing to sign their union contract. Council ratified the city's first-ever firefighter contract back in April 2025, but Dickens has let it sit unsigned for almost a year now. That's left the union in legal limbo and firefighters without the protections they thought they'd secured.

Both ICE resolutions head to the full council for a vote on April 20. The firefighter contract situation, meanwhile, continues to fester as a political headache for Dickens, who's caught between labor supporters who helped elect him and budget concerns about long-term commitments to city workers.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta firefighters and supporters at City Council meeting

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Makeover on West Howard Nearly Finished
stories.summary: The West Howard Avenue project between downtown Decatur and the city limits is wrapping up after starting in August. The mile-long stretch now features a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming improvements. The project connects Commerce Drive near Decatur High School to Paden Circle by East Lake MARTA station.
stories.body:
After nearly a decade of planning, Decatur's big Complete Streets experiment on West Howard Avenue is almost done. The project transformed about a mile of what used to be a high-speed car corridor into something that actually works for people walking and biking. The two-way cycle track with concrete barriers is the centerpiece, but the sidewalk and traffic calming improvements might be just as important.

The route connects downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA, which makes it more than just a nice amenity for recreational cyclists. This is actual transportation infrastructure that gives people a real alternative to driving for trips between Decatur and the rest of the metro. That concrete barrier protection is key, too, because West Howard carries enough traffic that painted bike lanes would be useless.

What's interesting is watching how Complete Streets projects like this perform once they're finished. If this works well and gets good usage, it becomes a model for other corridors in Decatur and neighboring cities. If it doesn't, it becomes ammunition for people who think bike infrastructure is a waste of money. Early signs look promising though, with the infrastructure quality high enough to attract riders who wouldn't feel safe in typical Atlanta bike lanes.
stories.imageAlt: New concrete-protected bike lanes on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: ICE Agents Show Up at Hartsfield During TSA Chaos
stories.summary: Federal immigration officers are patrolling the airport in tactical gear to provide security as the partial government shutdown creates massive delays. TSA agents working without pay have been calling out at record rates, with 41% absent on Sunday. The airport is telling travelers to arrive four hours early for domestic flights.
stories.body:
The scenes at Hartsfield this week have been pretty surreal. Armed ICE agents in full tactical gear are patrolling the terminals while TSA lines stretch for hours and frustrated travelers sleep on the floor. It's what happens when a partial government shutdown meets the world's busiest airport during spring travel season.

Mayor Dickens was quick to clarify that these ICE agents aren't doing immigration enforcement, they're just providing general security while TSA agents continue working without paychecks. But having armed federal immigration officers walking around the airport sends a pretty clear message about federal priorities, regardless of their stated role.

The real story is the human cost of this mess. TSA agents are calling out at record rates because they can't afford to work without pay, and travelers are spending entire nights in security lines just trying to get home. The airport is recommending four-hour arrival times for domestic flights, which is absolutely insane for what's supposed to be efficient air travel infrastructure.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents in tactical gear at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Someone's 8-Foot Python Showed Up on the BeltLine
stories.summary: A video captured a man handling what appears to be an 8-10 foot python found along the BeltLine. Wildlife officials are warning against handling unknown snakes, and it's unclear if the person in the video was the owner or just a random guy who decided to wrestle a massive snake on the trail.
stories.body:
Just another Tuesday on the BeltLine, right? Someone spotted and filmed what looks like an 8-10 foot python hanging out in the bushes, and then some guy just walks up and starts yanking it out like he's dealing with a garden hose. Joseph Ragland caught the whole thing on video and had the good sense to stay back while this impromptu wildlife rescue was happening.

Here's the thing though: nobody knows if the guy handling the snake was actually the owner or just some fearless soul who decided to help. Either way, wildlife experts are pretty concerned about people handling unknown snakes, especially ones that could potentially be dangerous. Even if it's someone's escaped pet, you don't know if it's been injured or stressed.

This is actually becoming more of a thing as Atlanta gets denser. People keep exotic pets, sometimes those pets escape, and they end up in the most random places. The BeltLine, with all its foot traffic and visibility, seems to be where a lot of these encounters happen. Pro tip: if you see a massive snake on the trail, maybe call animal control instead of trying to become the neighborhood hero.
